summary: I graduated from the University of California in 1912, which was the starting point of my career in mining law. The education I received there laid the foundation for my interest in the legal aspects of mining operations. It was through this background that I gradually became involved with various mining companies over the years.

Before joining the U.S. Potash Company, I worked at the National Park Service, which shaped my views on conservation and responsible resource management. I left the National Park Service in 1933 to become president of the U.S. Potash Company, marking a significant career shift. My experience there gave me a unique perspective on balancing environmental concerns with economic development in mining.

In 1933, I became president of the U.S. Potash Company, which was a major change from my previous conservation work. One of my main challenges was modernizing operations and improving labor relations, including efforts to reduce the workweek to fifty-two hours and hiring Hispanic workers. Navigating mining unions and maintaining good relations was also a critical part of my role there.

Later in my career, I led the U.S. Borax and Chemical Corporation, where we faced difficult economic times but managed to maintain production through innovation and strong community ties among workers. Labor negotiations were delicate, and avoiding strikes was essential to the company's stability. This leadership role allowed me to further implement responsible mining practices and fair labor policies.
